modules.title: IELTS Foundations
modules.description: Understand the IELTS exam structure and build your base

lessons.title: What is IELTS?
lessons.theory:
IELTS (International English Language Testing System) has two versions: **Academic** (for university) and **General Training** (for work/migration). The test has 4 sections:

1. **Listening** – 30 min, 40 questions. You hear recordings and answer questions.
2. **Reading** – 60 min, 40 questions. Read 3 long passages and answer questions.
3. **Writing** – 60 min, 2 tasks. Task 1: describe a graph/chart (150 words). Task 2: write an essay (250 words).
4. **Speaking** – 11-14 min, face-to-face with an examiner.

Your score is a **Band Score** from 1.0 to 9.0. For most universities, you need Band 6.0–7.0.
key_points: 4 skills tested: Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking
lessons.tips: Start with the Academic version if you're going to university. General Training is slightly easier for Reading and Writing.

lessons.title: Band Scores Explained
lessons.theory:
**Band Score Guide:**

- **Band 3.5–4.0** (Your current level – Pre-intermediate): Very limited vocabulary, many grammar errors, basic ideas only.
- **Band 4.5–5.0**: You can communicate simple ideas but make frequent errors.
- **Band 5.0–5.5**: Partial competence, can handle basic situations.
- **Band 6.0–6.5**: Generally effective, some inaccuracies.
- **Band 7.0+**: Good user, handles complex language well.

**Your Goal:** Move from 3.5 → 5.5 (beginner target) → 6.0+ (advanced target)

Each 0.5 band improvement requires consistent practice for 1-3 months.
key_points: All 4 sections are averaged for final score
lessons.tips: Don't get discouraged by your starting band! Every expert was once a beginner. Focus on one skill at a time.

lessons.title: Study Plan & Mindset
lessons.theory:
**The 12-Week IELTS Plan (Pre-Intermediate):**

**Weeks 1-3:** Grammar foundations + Basic vocabulary (500 words)
**Weeks 4-6:** Listening skills + Reading strategies
**Weeks 7-9:** Writing Task 1 & 2 fundamentals
**Weeks 10-12:** Speaking practice + Full mock tests

**Daily routine (45-60 min):**
- Morning (15 min): Vocabulary review
- Study session (30 min): Lesson or skill practice
- Evening (10 min): Review what you learned

**Free Resources to Use:**
- BBC Learning English (bbc.co.uk/learningenglish)
- British Council LearnEnglish
- IELTS.org official practice materials
- YouTube: IELTS Liz, IELTS Simon
key_points: Use free BBC and British Council resources
lessons.tips: Consistency beats intensity! 45 minutes every day is better than 5 hours on weekends.

modules.title: Grammar Essentials
modules.description: Master the grammar structures IELTS examiners look for

lessons.title: Tense Review: Present & Past
lessons.theory:
**Present Simple** – facts, habits, routines
- 'Water *boils* at 100°C.' / 'I *study* English every day.'

**Present Continuous** – happening now or temporary
- 'The graph *is showing* a sharp increase.' / 'I *am improving* my English.'

**Past Simple** – completed actions
- 'The population *increased* in 2010.' / 'I *visited* London last year.'

**Present Perfect** – past actions connected to now
- 'The city *has grown* significantly since 2000.' / 'I *have studied* for three hours.'

**⭐ IELTS Writing Tip:** Use Present Perfect to describe trends in graphs that continue to the present!
key_points: Present Perfect links past to present
lessons.tips: In Writing Task 1, use 'has increased', 'have risen' when the trend continues to today.

lessons.title: Conditionals for Speaking & Writing
lessons.theory:
Conditionals show conditions and results. IELTS examiners love them!

**Zero Conditional** – general truths
- 'If you *heat* ice, it *melts*.'

**First Conditional** – real/possible future
- 'If the government *invests* in education, crime *will decrease*.'

**Second Conditional** – unreal/hypothetical
- 'If I *had* more time, I *would practice* speaking every day.'

**Third Conditional** – past hypothetical
- 'If they *had built* more hospitals, fewer people *would have died*.'

**⭐ Speaking Tip:** Use 2nd conditional to speculate: 'If I *were* the government, I *would* invest in renewable energy.' This shows advanced grammar to the examiner!
lessons.tips: A common mistake: DON'T say 'If I would have...' — it should be 'If I had...'

lessons.title: Passive Voice
lessons.theory:
The passive voice is essential for IELTS Writing Task 1 (describing processes)!

**Structure:** Subject + **be** (conjugated) + **past participle**

**Active:** 'The company *produces* 500 cars a day.'
**Passive:** '500 cars *are produced* a day.'

**Tense examples:**
- Present: 'The data *is collected* monthly.'
- Past: 'The factory *was built* in 1990.'
- Future: 'The report *will be published* next year.'
- Present Perfect: 'New laws *have been introduced*.'

**When to use passive:**
✅ When the action is more important than WHO did it
✅ In academic/formal writing
✅ To describe processes (Task 1)
✅ To sound more objective
lessons.tips: Look for irregular past participles: write→written, make→made, build→built, break→broken

lessons.title: Linking Words & Cohesion
lessons.theory:
Linking words improve your **Coherence & Cohesion** score (25% of Writing score)!

**Adding ideas:**
- Furthermore, Moreover, In addition, Additionally, Also

**Contrasting:**
- However, Nevertheless, On the other hand, Despite this, Although

**Cause & Effect:**
- Therefore, As a result, Consequently, Due to, Because of

**Sequencing (for processes):**
- First, Then, Next, Subsequently, Finally, After that

**Giving examples:**
- For example, For instance, Such as, To illustrate

**Concluding:**
- In conclusion, To summarize, Overall, In summary

**⚠️ Common mistake:** Don't overuse 'However'! Vary your linking words.
key_points: Use variety — don't repeat same linkers
Always begin Task 2 conclusion with 'In conclusion'
lessons.tips: Learn 2-3 linkers from each category. Using them correctly is better than using many incorrectly.

modules.title: IELTS Listening
modules.description: Strategies to score higher in the Listening test

lessons.title: Listening Test Structure
lessons.theory:
The IELTS Listening test has **4 sections, 40 questions, 30 minutes** (+ 10 min to transfer answers on paper).

**Section 1:** Everyday conversation (e.g., booking a hotel room) — EASIEST
**Section 2:** Monologue about a local topic (e.g., town guide) — EASY
**Section 3:** Academic discussion between 2-4 people — MEDIUM
**Section 4:** Academic lecture or talk — HARDEST

**Question types:**
- Multiple choice
- Form/note/table completion
- Matching
- Labelling a map or diagram
- Short answer questions

**Key rule:** The answers appear **in order** in the audio. If you miss one, move on!
lessons.tips: Sections 1 and 2 are your opportunity for easy marks. Focus hard here first!

lessons.title: Prediction & Keyword Strategies
lessons.theory:
**The #1 strategy: Read questions BEFORE you listen!**

You always get time before each section starts. Use it to:
1. **Underline keywords** in questions (nouns, verbs, adjectives)
2. **Predict the answer type** (a number? a name? a day?)
3. **Predict synonyms** — the recording will often use DIFFERENT words from the question!

**Example:**
Question: 'The library opens at _____ on Sundays.'
→ Predict: a TIME (9am, 10:30am)
→ Keywords: library, opens, Sunday
→ Synonym: The recording might say 'Sunday hours begin at...'

**Spelling matters!** Check spelling for names, places, and numbers written as words.

**Practice:** BBC Learning English has free audio with scripts.
key_points: Watch for synonyms in the audio
lessons.tips: If you write 'recieve' instead of 'receive' — you lose the mark! Practice spelling common IELTS words.

lessons.title: Numbers, Dates & Spelling
lessons.theory:
These are the most common reasons students lose marks!

**Numbers:**
- Write figures: 15 (not 'fifteen') unless asked otherwise
- Prices: £45.50 (note the decimal)
- Phone: 07700 900123 (write exactly as said)

**Dates:**
- 15th March / March 15 / 15/03 — all are acceptable
- 'the fifteenth of March' → write: 15 March

**Commonly misspelled IELTS words:**
- accommodation (not accomodation)
- necessary (one c, two s)
- government (not goverment)
- environment (not enviroment)
- February (not Febuary)
- Wednesday (not Wendsday)
- definitely (not definately)

**Practice:** BBC Sounds has free radio programs to improve listening.
key_points: Dates: any clear format is ok
lessons.tips: Keep a personal list of words you misspell. Review it daily for 5 minutes!

modules.title: IELTS Reading
modules.description: Read faster and smarter — answer 40 questions in 60 minutes

lessons.title: Skimming & Scanning
lessons.theory:
You have **60 minutes for 40 questions** — that's only 90 seconds per question!

You MUST use two key skills:

**Skimming** = reading quickly for the MAIN IDEA
- Read the title, headings, first sentence of each paragraph
- Takes 2-3 minutes per passage
- Gives you a 'map' of the text

**Scanning** = searching for SPECIFIC INFORMATION
- Look for keywords, numbers, names, dates
- Your eyes move quickly, not word-by-word
- Used to locate the answer's paragraph

**Reading strategy:**
1. Skim the passage (2-3 min)
2. Read the questions
3. Scan to find the relevant paragraph
4. Read that section carefully to get the answer

**Time allocation:** 20 min per passage
lessons.tips: NEVER read the whole passage word-for-word before looking at questions — you won't have time!

lessons.title: True/False/Not Given
lessons.theory:
This is one of the most confusing question types for many students!

**True:** The statement AGREES with what the text says
**False:** The statement CONTRADICTS the text (the text says the opposite)
**Not Given:** The information is NOT in the text at all

**The key difference — False vs Not Given:**
- **False** = The text says something DIFFERENT or OPPOSITE
- **Not Given** = The text doesn't mention it AT ALL

**Example text:** 'The museum was built in 1890 and attracts 50,000 visitors annually.'

- 'The museum was built in the 19th century.' → **TRUE** (1890 is in the 1800s)
- 'The museum was built in 1895.' → **FALSE** (text says 1890, not 1895)
- 'The museum is the most popular in the city.' → **NOT GIVEN** (nothing about 'most popular')

**Rule:** Only use information FROM THE TEXT. Your own knowledge = wrong answer!
lessons.tips: If you're unsure between False and Not Given — ask: 'Does the text say the OPPOSITE?' If yes → False. If the topic isn't mentioned → Not Given.

lessons.title: Matching Headings
lessons.theory:
In Matching Headings, you match headings from a list to paragraphs in the text.

**Strategy:**
1. Read the headings list first
2. Skim the first paragraph — identify its MAIN IDEA (usually in the first 1-2 sentences)
3. Find the heading that matches the main idea
4. Cross out the heading once used (unless told otherwise)

**Common traps:**
- A heading might contain a word from the paragraph but NOT be the main idea
- The main idea is usually in the TOPIC SENTENCE (first sentence)
- Sometimes the last sentence summarizes the main idea

**Example:**
Paragraph: 'Solar energy has seen dramatic growth. The cost of solar panels fell 90% in the last decade, making it competitive with fossil fuels...'

Correct heading: 'The falling cost of renewable energy' ✅
Wrong heading: 'Problems with fossil fuels' ❌ (mentioned but not the main point)
key_points: Find main idea in topic sentence
lessons.tips: The longest paragraphs often have the most obvious headings. Start with those to gain confidence!

modules.title: IELTS Writing
modules.description: Write essays and reports that impress IELTS examiners

lessons.title: Task 1: Describing Graphs
lessons.theory:
**Task 1 (20 min, 150 words):** Describe a visual (graph, chart, diagram, map)

**4-paragraph structure:**
1. **Introduction:** Paraphrase the question (DON'T copy it!)
2. **Overview:** The 2-3 most important features (no numbers here!)
3. **Body 1:** Key details with data
4. **Body 2:** More details / comparisons

**Vocabulary for trends:**
📈 Increase: rose, grew, increased, went up, climbed, surged
📉 Decrease: fell, dropped, declined, decreased, went down, plummeted
➡️ No change: remained stable, stayed constant, levelled off
📊 High point: peaked at, reached a peak of
📊 Low point: bottomed out at, fell to a low of

**With adverbs:** sharply, dramatically, significantly, gradually, slightly, steadily

**Example:** 'The number of students *rose sharply* from 200 to 450 between 2010 and 2020.'
lessons.tips: The Overview is THE most important paragraph — it shows you can identify key trends. Many students skip it and lose marks!

lessons.title: Task 2: Essay Writing
lessons.theory:
**Task 2 (40 min, 250 words):** Write an opinion/discussion/problem essay

**5-paragraph structure:**
1. **Introduction** (2-3 sentences): Background sentence + Thesis statement
2. **Body 1** (4-5 sentences): First main argument + example/evidence
3. **Body 2** (4-5 sentences): Second main argument + example/evidence
4. **Body 3** (optional): Counter-argument or third point
5. **Conclusion** (2-3 sentences): Restate thesis + summary

**Introduction template:**
'In recent years, [topic] has become a significant issue. While some people believe [view A], others argue [view B]. This essay will discuss [what you'll argue].'

**Body paragraph template (PEEL):**
- **P**oint: State your argument
- **E**xample: Give a real-world example
- **E**xplanation: Explain how it supports your point
- **L**ink: Connect back to the question

**Task 2 is worth TWICE as much as Task 1!**
key_points: Task 2 = double the marks of Task 1
Use PEEL structure for body paragraphs
lessons.tips: Spend 5 minutes PLANNING before writing. Students who plan write better essays and don't go off-topic.

lessons.title: Common Writing Mistakes
lessons.theory:
**Top 10 IELTS Writing mistakes (and how to fix them):**

1. ❌ Copying the question word-for-word → ✅ Paraphrase using synonyms
2. ❌ Writing less than 150/250 words → ✅ Always count your words
3. ❌ No paragraphs / one big block → ✅ Indent each new paragraph
4. ❌ Using informal language ('gonna', 'kids', 'lots of') → ✅ Use formal vocabulary
5. ❌ No overview in Task 1 → ✅ Always write 'Overall...' paragraph
6. ❌ Using 'I think' repeatedly → ✅ 'It is argued that...', 'Many people believe...'
7. ❌ Subject-verb disagreement → ✅ 'The number of people IS' (not 'are')
8. ❌ Starting every sentence with 'I' → ✅ Use passive voice and impersonal phrases
9. ❌ Memorized phrases that don't fit → ✅ Natural, relevant language only
10. ❌ Not checking your work → ✅ Save 2 min at end to check spelling/grammar
lessons.tips: Avoid contractions in writing: DON'T use 'it's', 'can't', 'won't' — write 'it is', 'cannot', 'will not'.

modules.title: IELTS Speaking
modules.description: Speak confidently and fluently in your IELTS exam

lessons.title: Speaking Test Structure
lessons.theory:
The Speaking test is **11-14 minutes**, face-to-face with a real examiner.

**Part 1 (4-5 min):** Everyday questions about yourself
- Topics: home, family, work/study, hobbies, food, travel
- Answers should be 2-4 sentences — not too short, not too long!

**Part 2 (3-4 min):** Long turn — you speak for 1-2 minutes
- You get a topic card and 1 minute to prepare (take notes!)
- Speak continuously — the examiner won't ask questions

**Part 3 (4-5 min):** Discussion — abstract questions related to Part 2 topic
- More complex opinions needed
- Use conditionals, speculation, and complex structures

**You are scored on:**
- Fluency & Coherence (25%)
- Lexical Resource / Vocabulary (25%)
- Grammatical Range & Accuracy (25%)
- Pronunciation (25%)
lessons.tips: The examiner is NOT trying to trick you — they want you to succeed! Speak naturally and don't worry about a slight accent.

lessons.title: Expanding Your Answers
lessons.theory:
The biggest mistake in IELTS Speaking: **giving too-short answers!**

**The AREA technique:**
- **A**nswer the question directly
- **R**eason — explain why
- **E**xample — give a personal example
- **A**lternative — mention an alternative view or add more detail

**Example:**
Question: 'Do you like cooking?'
❌ Bad: 'Yes, I like it.'
✅ Good: 'Yes, I really enjoy cooking, especially on weekends. **The main reason is** that it helps me relax after a stressful week. **For instance**, last Saturday I made a traditional Uzbek dish for my family and everyone loved it. **I also find** that cooking at home is much healthier than eating out.'

**Useful phrases to buy thinking time:**
- 'That's an interesting question...'
- 'Let me think about that for a moment...'
- 'Well, from my perspective...'
- 'I've never really thought about it, but I'd say...'
key_points: Use AREA: Answer, Reason, Example, Alternative
lessons.tips: Record yourself speaking and listen back. You'll quickly notice your most common mistakes and improvements needed!

lessons.title: Pronunciation & Fluency
lessons.theory:
**Pronunciation does NOT mean having a perfect British accent.** It means being understood clearly.

**Key pronunciation features examiners assess:**
1. **Word stress:** 'phoTOgraphy' not 'PHOtography'
2. **Sentence stress:** Content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) are stressed, not function words
3. **Connected speech:** 'want to' → 'wanna', 'going to' → 'gonna' (in natural speech)
4. **Intonation:** Rising for questions, falling for statements

**Improving fluency:**
- Talk to yourself in English daily (narrate what you're doing!)
- Repeat BBC or TED Talk sentences out loud
- Shadowing: Listen and speak simultaneously
- Don't translate from your language — think directly in English

**Fluency killers to avoid:**
- Long silences (use 'thinking' phrases instead)
- Repeating the same word
- Starting over when you make a mistake

**Free practice:** italki.com (find free language exchange partners)
lessons.tips: Watch 10 minutes of English TV with English subtitles every day. Your ear and pronunciation will improve dramatically!
